pages.title: Saved YouTube Video
pages.subtitle: AI Flipable Book
pages.body:
[0:04] What's up geeks and welcome to the channel! In the first video of this series we are going
[0:10] to start with one of the simplest yet one of the most used design
[0:14] patterns out there: The Singleton Pattern. The singleton is a creational design pattern
[0:20] this pattern ensures that only one instance of its kind exists
[0:24] and provides a single point of access to it from any other part of our application
[0:30] As a real-world analogy take the government of a country or its president,
[0:34] you see a given country can have only one official government or one official president
[0:39] regardless of the individuals who form this government or the person that
[0:43] is currently the president of this country, so the title "the government or the president
[0:48] of X" is a global identifier it is a global point of access
[0:53] used to represent or reference the president...
keyTakeaways: [0:04] What's up geeks and welcome to the channel

In the first video of this series we are going
[0:10] to start with one of the simplest yet one of the most used design
[0:14] patterns out there: The Singleton Pattern

The singleton is a creational design pattern
[0:20] this pattern ensures that only one instance of its kind exists
[0:24] and provides a single point of access to it from any other part of our application
[0:30] As a real-world analogy take the government of a country or its president,
[0:34] you see a given country can have only one official government or one official president
[0:39] regardless of the individuals who form this government or the person that
[0:43] is currently the president of this country, so the title "the government or the president
[0:48] of X" is a global identifier it is a global point of access
[0:53] used to represent or reference the president or the individuals in charge of this country

[0:58] Singletons represent exactly the same idea, just like global variables they let you access
[1:04] your object from anywhere in your application but on top of that, they encapsulate the
[1:09] attributes of this object in one single class and guarantee that only one instance of this class
[1:16] will be available at any point in time but why would anyone want to control
[1:21] how many instances a class has
pages.misconceptionFix:
Well think of a database instance,
[1:26] only one instance of a database is required and should be used by all the parts of our application
[1:31] So instead of creating a new instance every time we need to query our database
[1:36] we create a singleton database instance and reuse this same instance every time we access our tables
[1:42] by doing this we didn't only guarantee a faster access and retrieval of the instance
[1:47] but we also reduced the possibility of encountering an error or exception
[1:52] while creating these several instances
pages.didYouKnow:
In the first video of this series we are going
[0:10] to start with one of the simplest yet one of the most used design
[0:14] patterns out there: The Singleton Pattern
pages.example:
The singleton is a creational design pattern
[0:20] this pattern ensures that only one instance of its kind exists
[0:24] and provides a single point of access to it from any other part of our application
[0:30] As a real-world analogy take the government of a country or its president,
[0:34] you see a given country can have only one official government or one official president
[0:39] regardless of the individuals who form this government or the person that
[0:43] is currently the president of this country, so the title "the government or the president
[0:48] of X" is a global identifier it is a global point of access
[0:53] used to represent or reference the president or the individuals in charge of this country

pages.title: Saved YouTube Video
pages.subtitle: Grounded lesson page
pages.body:
[3:35] and to these people I say you're right! To solve this issue and as discussed
[3:40] in our previous videos, all we have to do is wrap
[3:43] our if-statement with a synchronized block Now every time a thread tries to
[3:47] create an instance of the Singleton class it will have to wait for its turn as one thread
[3:52] only may execute this code block at a given time. Okay with this we solved the threading issue
[3:58] however we added unnecessary overhead to the creation process of our singleton object.
[4:04] You see after the single instance is created, when the threads of our application try to
[4:08] access it they will first have to wait for their turn to acquire the lock on our class
[4:13] and only when it is their time to go they will check if the instance
[4:16] is available and then return with it This problem is actually not specific
[4:21] to the Singleton pattern and...
keyTakeaways: [3:35] and to these people I say you're right

To solve this issue and as discussed
[3:40] in our previous videos, all we have to do is wrap
[3:43] our if-statement with a synchronized block Now every time a thread tries to
[3:47] create an instance of the Singleton class it will have to wait for its turn as one thread
[3:52] only may execute this code block at a given time

Okay with this we solved the threading issue
[3:58] however we added unnecessary overhead to the creation process of our singleton object

[4:04] You see after the single instance is created, when the threads of our application try to
[4:08] access it they will first have to wait for their turn to acquire the lock on our class
[4:13] and only when it is their time to go they will check if the instance
[4:16] is available and then return with it This problem is actually not specific
[4:21] to the Singleton pattern and can be solved by applying
[4:24] the double-checked locking idiom on our method How do we do that
pages.misconceptionFix:
Well, we simply wrap
[4:31] the synchronized block with another if statement similar to the one inside it
[4:35] I know the code may look confusing at first but look closely
[4:39] now the threads trying to retrieve the instance of our Singleton class after being instantiated
[4:44] do not necessarily have to acquire the lock on our class
[4:48] as we are first testing the locking criteria what we did improved the performance of our
[4:54] code by limiting synchronization to the rare case of computing the field's value
[5:00] or constructing a new instance of this field and by skipping synchronization during the
[5:05] common cases of retrieving an already created instance or value of this field
pages.didYouKnow:
To solve this issue and as discussed
[3:40] in our previous videos, all we have to do is wrap
[3:43] our if-statement with a synchronized block Now every time a thread tries to
[3:47] create an instance of the Singleton class it will have to wait for its turn as one thread
[3:52] only may execute this code block at a given time
pages.example:
Okay with this we solved the threading issue
[3:58] however we added unnecessary overhead to the creation process of our singleton object

items: [3:35] and to these people I say you're right

To solve this issue and as discussed
[3:40] in our previous videos, all we have to do is wrap
[3:43] our if-statement with a synchronized block Now every time a thread tries to
[3:47] create an instance of the Singleton class it will have to wait for its turn as one thread
[3:52] only may execute this code block at a given time

Okay with this we solved the threading issue
[3:58] however we added unnecessary overhead to the creation process of our singleton object

[4:04] You see after the single instance is created, when the threads of our application try to
[4:08] access it they will first have to wait for their turn to acquire the lock on our class
[4:13] and only when it is their time to go they will check if the instance
[4:16] is available and then return with it This problem is actually not specific
[4:21] to the Singleton pattern and can be solved by applying
[4:24] the double-checked locking idiom on our method How do we do that

pages.subtitle: Grounded lesson page
pages.body:
[6:56] now marked as volatile so every time we access
[6:59] this variable we need to read it directly from the main memory as it cannot be cached
[7:03] So in this single code snippet even if the variable was already initialized
[7:09] we have to fetch it twice the first time in the
[7:12] if-statement to check if it is null and the second time in the return
[7:15] statement to return it to the user. To avoid doing this and access the
[7:19] memory only once what we can do is
[7:21] store this instance in a local variable the first time we retrieve it from the memory
[7:26] then use this same local variable in both the if-statement and the return statement.
[7:31] The same logic and way of thinking can be expanded and used inside the synchronized block
[7:36] to avoid multiple direct reads of the memory. This simple usage of such a local variable
[7:42] can and will improve the methods...
keyTakeaways:
[6:56] now marked as volatile so every time we access
[6:59] this variable we need to read it directly from the main memory as it cannot be cached
[7:03] So in this single code snippet even if the variable was already initialized
[7:09] we have to fetch it twice the first time in the
[7:12] if-statement to check if it is null and the second time in the return
[7:15] statement to return it to the user

To avoid doing this and access the
[7:19] memory only once what we can do is
[7:21] store this instance in a local variable the first time we retrieve it from the memory
[7:26] then use this same local variable in both the if-statement and the return statement

[7:31] The same logic and way of thinking can be expanded and used inside the synchronized block
[7:36] to avoid multiple direct reads of the memory

This simple usage of such a local variable
[7:42] can and will improve the methods overall performance by as much as 40%
[7:48] So to recap we can say that the Singleton Pattern should be used when a class must have a single
[7:54] instance available to all its clients as this pattern disables all other means
[7:59] of creating objects of a class except for the special static creation method
[8:04] This method either creates a new object or returns the existing one if it has already been created
[8:10] However this pattern requires special treatment in a multi-threaded environment as its code needs
[8:15] to be adapted to handle multiple threads
pages.misconceptionFix:
So that's it for this video, I hope it was
[8:21] helpful, thank you guys for watching, take care, and I will see you in the next one!
pages.didYouKnow:
To avoid doing this and access the
[7:19] memory only once what we can do is
[7:21] store this instance in a local variable the first time we retrieve it from the memory
[7:26] then use this same local variable in both the if-statement and the return statement
pages.example:
[7:31] The same logic and way of thinking can be expanded and used inside the synchronized block
[7:36] to avoid multiple direct reads of the memory

items:
[6:56] now marked as volatile so every time we access
[6:59] this variable we need to read it directly from the main memory as it cannot be cached
[7:03] So in this single code snippet even if the variable was already initialized
[7:09] we have to fetch it twice the first time in the
[7:12] if-statement to check if it is null and the second time in the return
[7:15] statement to return it to the user

To avoid doing this and access the
[7:19] memory only once what we can do is
[7:21] store this instance in a local variable the first time we retrieve it from the memory
[7:26] then use this same local variable in both the if-statement and the return statement

[7:31] The same logic and way of thinking can be expanded and used inside the synchronized block
[7:36] to avoid multiple direct reads of the memory

This simple usage of such a local variable
[7:42] can and will improve the methods overall performance by as much as 40%
[7:48] So to recap we can say that the Singleton Pattern should be used when a class must have a single
[7:54] instance available to all its clients as this pattern disables all other means
[7:59] of creating objects of a class except for the special static creation method
[8:04] This method either creates a new object or returns the existing one if it has already been created
[8:10] However this pattern requires special treatment in a multi-threaded environment as its code needs
[8:15] to be adapted to handle multiple threads

summary: The Singleton Pattern is a creational design pattern that guarantees only one instance of a class exists throughout the application, providing a single, controlled point of access to that instance. The resource details the implementation steps, starting from basic private constructors and progressing through advanced fixes required for multi-threaded environments, including using `volatile` and local variables for optimal performance.
